title: Listening - Part 3: Why We Buy What We Don't Need
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a behavioral economist, Thomas, and a retail consultant, Sonia, about the hidden forces behind our shopping habits. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring 'choice architecture,' the 'scarcity principle,' and how physical environments dictate spending.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We like to think of ourselves as rational shoppers, but is that really the case? Joining me are Thomas, an expert in behavioral economics, and Sonia, who designs store layouts. Thomas, why do we often walk into a shop for milk and leave with a full trolley?

Thomas: [15] It’s because our 'rational' brain is easily overwhelmed by 'choice architecture.' When we have too many options, we experience 'decision fatigue.' [/15] Retailers know this. They use 'nudges'—subtle cues that guide us toward certain products without us even realizing it. We think we are making a free choice, but the environment has already narrowed the field for us.

Interviewer: Sonia, you’re the one creating these environments. What’s the secret to keeping people in a store longer?

Sonia: [16] It’s about 'sensory branding.' We use specific scents, like freshly baked bread, or a certain tempo of music to slow people down. If you walk slower, you see more. [/16] We also place essential items, like milk or eggs, at the very back of the store. To get to them, you have to pass dozens of 'impulse buy' displays. It’s a physical gauntlet designed to tempt your 'System 1' brain—the fast, emotional side of your thinking.

Interviewer: Thomas, we’re seeing a lot of 'limited time offers' online. Does that actually work?

Thomas: [17] It’s incredibly powerful. This is the 'scarcity principle.' When we think something is running out, our brain perceives it as more valuable. It triggers a 'fear of missing out' or FOMO. [/17] Our ancestors had to act fast when food was scarce, and that biological hardware hasn't changed. A ticking clock on a website bypasses our logic and forces us into an emotional, hurried decision.

Interviewer: Sonia, is there a dark side to 'free' samples and loyalty cards?

Sonia: [18] It’s based on the 'law of reciprocity.' When someone gives you a free chocolate or a sample of perfume, you feel a subconscious obligation to give something back—usually by making a purchase. [/18] Loyalty cards do the same thing. They aren't just about discounts; they are about 'gamifying' the experience. You want to 'win' the points, even if you’re spending more than you save.

Interviewer: Thomas, can we ever truly 'hack' our own brains to stop overspending?

Thomas: [19] The best way is to 'add friction.' The modern world is designed to be 'frictionless'—think of one-click ordering or contactless payments. When it’s too easy to pay, the 'pain of paying' is removed. [/19] If you use cash or delete your saved card details, you force your rational brain to wake up and ask: 'Do I really need this?'

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Sonia? Is the future of shopping going to be even more manipulative?

Sonia: [20] It’s moving toward 'hyper-personalization.' With AI, stores will know exactly what you’re likely to buy before you do. The challenge for the consumer is to stay mindful in a world that is becoming increasingly 'predictive.' [/20]

Interviewer: Thomas, Sonia, thank you.

questions.question: Thomas explains that 'decision fatigue' occurs when
options: A) a person has to walk too far to find what they need.
B) the brain is presented with too many different choices.
C) a shopper is physically tired after a long day of work.
D) the store is too noisy for the customer to think.
questions.explanation: He says that when we have too many options, our rational brain is overwhelmed, causing 'decision fatigue'.

questions.question: Sonia uses 'sensory branding' primarily to
options: A) make the store smell better for the employees.
B) encourage customers to move through the store more slowly.
C) hide the fact that the products are of low quality.
D) distract customers from the high prices of the items.
questions.explanation: She states that scents and music are used to 'slow people down' because if they walk slower, they 'see more'.

questions.question: According to Thomas, the 'scarcity principle' is effective because
options: A) modern products are becoming harder to manufacture.
B) people enjoy the challenge of finding rare items.
C) it triggers an ancient biological instinct to act quickly.
D) it allows companies to charge much higher prices.
questions.explanation: He links it to our ancestors needing to act fast when food was scarce, which is still part of our 'biological hardware'.

questions.question: Sonia suggests that free samples work because they
options: A) prove that the company is generous and kind.
B) make the customer feel a duty to buy something in return.
C) allow the customer to test the quality of the product.
D) are cheaper than traditional television advertising.
questions.explanation: She cites the 'law of reciprocity', where receiving something for free creates a 'subconscious obligation' to give back.

questions.question: Thomas recommends 'adding friction' to the shopping process to
options: A) make the physical act of walking around the store harder.
B) force the brain to think more carefully about each purchase.
C) encourage people to use more high-tech payment methods.
D) help shops organize their inventory more efficiently.
questions.explanation: He says removing 'frictionless' payments forces the 'rational brain to wake up' and evaluate the need for the purchase.

questions.question: Sonia predicts that future shopping will be defined by
options: A) a return to small, local corner shops.
B) the complete disappearance of physical stores.
C) AI systems that anticipate a customer's specific needs.
D) a ban on all forms of psychological marketing.
questions.explanation: She mentions 'hyper-personalization' where AI knows what you will buy before you even do.
